coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dot modified mesoporous silica nanocarriers for redox-responsive controlled drug delivery and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Jian
Liu, Chang
Li, Xian
Liu, Jie
Di, Donghua
Zhang, Ying
Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a smart nanocarrier (MSNs-SS-CDPAA) is developed for redox-responsive controlled drug delivery and in vivo bioimaging by grafting fluorescent carbon dots to the surface of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) via disulfide bonds. The polyanion polymer poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) was used to prepare the carboxyl-abundant carbon dots (CDPAA) by hydrothermal polymerization. The negatively charged CDPAA were anchored to the openings of MSNs containing the disulfide bonds through amidation and were used as gatekeepers for trapping the drugs within the pores. The in vitro release results indicated that the prepared MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX showed highly redox-responsive drug release in pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 PBS. In addition, the redox-responsive release mechanism was studied by measurement of the Zeta potential and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The prepared MSNs-SS-CDPAA exhibited excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence properties. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) showed that MSNs-SS-CDPAA could emit blue, green and red fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 408, 488 and 561nm, respectively. In addition, MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited a high cellular uptake as shown by CDPAA imaging and a therapeutic effect on cancer cells by MTT assay. This study describes a novel strategy for simultaneously controlled drug delivery and real-time imaging to track the behavior of nanoparticles during tumor therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Redox-responsive
Controlled drug delivery
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading and redox-stimuli responsive drug release
The mechanism of redox-responsive drug release
The advantage of MSNs-SS-CDPAA after modification of CDPAA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The photos of the aqueous solution of CDPAA pretreated with different volume of APS in the bright field (A) and UV light at 360nm (B).
TEM images (A), XRD pattern (B), FT-IR spectra (C) and XPS spectra (D) of CDPAA.
(A) the excitation and emission spectra of CDPAA (upper inset, UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDPAA) and (B) PL emission spectra of CDPAA at different excitation wavelengths.
TEM of (A) MSNs and (B) MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles.
(A) The corresponding zeta potentials of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA and (B) the FT-IR spectra of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA.
(A) The TGA curves of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA and (B) the photos of MSNs-SS-CDPAA in the bright field and UV light at 360nm.
Cumulative release profiles of MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX in pH 5.0 PBS and pH 7.4 PBS with the addition of 10mM GSH or not.
(A) Change in the zeta potential of MSNs-SS-CDPAA incubated with 10mM GSH and (B) the supernatant PL intensity in the presence of GSH or not.
(A) Size distribution of MSNs and MSNs-SS-CDPAA; (B) Images of MSNs and MSNs-SS-CDPAA dispersed in distilled water and pH 7.4 PBS with a carrier concentration of 3mg/mL.
CLSM images of A549 cells pretreated with (A) MSNs, (B) MSNs-SS-CDPAA and (C) MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX for 2h at 37°C under different excitation wavelengths of 408, 488 and 561nm.
The cell viability of free DOX, MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX at different concentrations of DOX in A549 cells for (A) 24h and (B) 48h.
(A) Drugs loading process for redox-responsive MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX and (B) redox-responsive drug release in response to GSH.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the fluorescent CDPAA.
The N2 adsorption-desorption parameters of different functionalized MSNs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dot modified mesoporous silica nanocarriers for redox-responsive controlled drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a smart nanocarrier (MSNs-SS-CDPAA) is developed for redox-responsive controlled drug delivery and in vivo bioimaging by grafting fluorescent carbon dots to the surface of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) via disulfide bonds. The polyanion polymer poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) was used to prepare the carboxyl-abundant carbon dots (CDPAA) by hydrothermal polymerization. The negatively charged CDPAA were anchored to the openings of MSNs containing the disulfide bonds through amidation and were used as gatekeepers for trapping the drugs within the pores. The in vitro release results indicated that the prepared MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX showed highly redox-responsive drug release in pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 PBS. In addition, the redox-responsive release mechanism was studied by measurement of the Zeta potential and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The prepared MSNs-SS-CDPAA exhibited excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence properties. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) showed that MSNs-SS-CDPAA could emit blue, green and red fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 408, 488 and 561nm, respectively. In addition, MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited a high cellular uptake as shown by CDPAA imaging and a therapeutic effect on cancer cells by MTT assay. This study describes a novel strategy for simultaneously controlled drug delivery and real-time imaging to track the behavior of nanoparticles during tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has been a major public health problem worldwide for centuries and cancer-related mortality is increasing dramatically [1–3]. Current cancer treatments such as chemotherapy affect many parts of the body, leading to severe side-effects due to nonspecific interactions and premature leakage of chemotherapeutic drugs [4–6]. In order to reduce these side-effects and improve the therapeutic efficacy, the use of targeted drug delivery systems (DDSs) have received considerable attention for cancer treatment in the past few decades. However, targeted DDSs still have some limitations owing to the premature leaking of therapeutic drugs prior to reaching their specific target site. Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop an ideal DDS to deliver cytotoxic drugs to tumor cells without premature leakage and release therapeutic drugs rapidly after being taken up by tumor cells [7]. Recently, stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems (DDSs) have attracted increasing attention since they can encapsulate drugs effectively and avoid unwanted drug leakage during the drug delivery process.

Among the various nanocarriers explored, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) with a pore diameter of 2–50nm have unique advantages as drug carriers for stimuli-responsive DDS, such as a high surface area and large pore volume, a well-defined pore structure, an adjustable pore size, good biocompatibility and an easily functionalized surface [8]. In this regard, various MSNs based stimuli-responsive DDS involve various gatekeepers, such as biomacromolecules [9], polymers [10], macrocyclic compounds [11] and inorganic nanoparticles [12] with diverse stimuli, including pH-, redox potential-, temperature- and enzyme-responsiveness [9]. Among the various stimuli-responsive DDS, redox-responsive systems have received a great deal of attention, especially for cancer therapy. In addition, the redox potential has become a popular stimulus due to the marked difference in the concentration of glutathione (GSH) between the extracellular fluids (10μM) and intracellular fluids (1–10mM) [13,14]. Moreover, the cytosolic GSH concentration in many tumor cells is at least 3-fold higher than those in normal cells [15]. Hence, we have developed a redox-responsive MSNs to achieve site-specific drug delivery.

As a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted a great deal of attention recently due to their great potential in biological imaging/labeling and sensor design [16]. Compared with traditional heavy-metal based quantum dots, these functional CDs exhibit excellent photo-stability, good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility [17]. Moreover, CDs can be anchored to the entrance of MSNs to control the on-demand drug release since their diameter size ranges from 2nm to 10nm. Positively charged MSNs can aggregate markedly and be eliminated rapidly from the circulation owing to the strong interactions with serum components while, negatively charged nanoparticles have the capacity to prolong the blood circulation time during biomedical applications [18,19].

Based on the above theories, poly(acrylic acid) (PAA), a polyanion polymer, was used as the carbon source to prepare carboxyl-abundant CDPAA by the hydrothermal method. The negatively charged CDPAA were anchored to the openings of the terminal amino group of MSNs containing the disulfide bonds through an amidation reaction and were used as gatekeepers for trapping the drugs within the pores. When a high concentration of GSH was added to the release medium, the disulfide bonds between the CDPAA and MSNs carriers were cleaved, separating the gatekeepers from the MSNs. Carboxyl-abundant CDPAA was chosen for three reasons: (1) CDPAA is biocompatible and its size below 6nm allows it to block the outlets of MSNs; (2) fluorescent CDPAA could serve as an imaging agent during the transport process; (3) the negatively charged CDPAA has the ability to prolong the circulation time and increase the biocompatibility and physiological stability of MSNs when administered intravenously [17].

In the present work, carboxyl-abundant carbon dots (CDPAA) with a diameter of 3nm were prepared by hydrothermal polymerization method. MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanocarriers were developed and found to have great potential for redox-responsive drug release at tumor sites and real-time imaging to monitor the cellular behavior during cancer treatment, where the CDPAA was grafted onto the surface of MSNs via disulfide bonds as described in Scheme 1 . Doxorubicin (DOX) was used as a model drug to evaluate the redox-responsive drug release of MSNs-SS-CDPAA. Also, the redox-responsive release mechanism was studied by measuring the Zeta change of MSNs-SS-CDPAA and the supernatant fluorescence intensity in the presence or absence of GSH. Stability and hemolytic assays were used to evaluate the biocompatibility of MSNs-SS-CDPAA. CLSM was used to evaluate the fluorescence and location of the nanocomposites with regard to A549 cells. Furthermore, MTT assay was used to estimate the therapeutic effect of DOX-loaded MSNs-SS-CDPAA. In a word, this MSNs-SS-CDPAA not only served as a drug delivery system to realize controlled drug delivery but is also promising for monitoring the cellular real-time imaging of carriers during cancer treatment.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS, 95%), cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB), cysteamine hydrochloride, 2,2′-dipyridyl disulfide, poly(acrylic acid) (PAA, Mw=2000), glutathione (GSH), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Cell culture medium dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25%), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin-streptomycin were obtained from GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, NM, USA). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were supplied by Amresco (USA). Fluorescent 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was obtained from Biyuntian Co. (Nanjing, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

PAA was first dissolved in deionized water at a concentration of 10mg/mL. Then different volumes (0mL, 0.15mL, 0.5mL, 1.5mL and 5mL) of 1M ammonium persulfate (APS) were added at 25°C for 1h. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10h at a heating rate of 5°C/min. After the reaction, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 16,000rpm to remove the larger particles and the supernatant was purified by dialysis (Mw=3500) against deionized water for 1day.

The MSNs nanoparticles were prepared according to the published literature [20]. 1.00g CTAB was dissolved in 480mL distilled water under vigorous stirring, and then 3.50mL 2M NaOH (aq) was added. After the mixture was heated to 80°C for 15min, 5.00mL TEOS was added dropwise to the surfactant CTAB solution. Then, the solution was further stirred for 2h and the resulting product was homogenized in a homogenizer at 800 bars for 5 cycles to reduce the aggregation of nanoparticles (AH100D, ATS Engineer Inc., Shanghai, China). The homogenized nanoparticles were centrifuged, and washed with ethanol and then the introduction of the thiol group was carried out by the post-grafting method using a dispersion of MSNs nanoparticles in 60mL ethanol, followed by the addition of MPTMS (0.8mL). The mixture was refluxed at 70°C under N2 for 12h and the thiol-functionalized MSNs (MSNs-SH) were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol. To remove the template CTAB, the product was refluxed twice in a solution of 18mL HCl (37%) and 320mL methanol for 12h, and then the samples were centrifuged, then washed and dried under vacuum.

The synthesis of S-(2-Aminoethylthio)-2-thiopyridine hydrochloride (Py-SS-NH2) was performed according to our previous study [21]. MSNs-SH (200mg) was first dispersed in 10mL ethanol, followed by the addition of Py-SS-NH2 (50mg). The mixture was stirred at 25°C for 36h and the resultant MSNs-SS-NH2 was separated by centrifugation, washed 3 times with ethanol, and dried in a vacuum for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and redox-stimuli responsive drug release
ce:section.ce:para: 20mg DOX was dissolved in 5mL distilled water and 5mL pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Then, 80mg MSNs-SH was added to the DOX solution, followed by sonication for 1h, then stirred for 12h at 25°C. The DOX-loaded MSNs-SH is referred to as MSNs-SH/DOX. The MSNs-SH/DOX was centrifuged and washed with PBS to remove the adsorbed DOX on the surface of the MSNs, then the DOX-loaded sample was dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS, and 50mg Py-SS-NH2 was added to the mixture followed by stirring for a further 24h. The mixture was then centrifuged and washed with PBS to obtain DOX-loaded MSNs-SS-NH2. Finally, 50mg CDPAA was dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS and then activated with 3mg/mL EDC and 2mg/mL NHS for 1h. Subsequently, DOX-loaded MSNs-SS-NH2 was added dropwise to the CDPAA solution and the reaction mixture was stirred for 12h. The DOX-loaded MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles are referred to as MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX. The resulting MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX was collected by centrifugation, and washed with pH 7.4 PBS. The supernatant was collected at each step to measure the amount of drug loading by UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 480nm. The total amount of DOX loaded in the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX was obtained by subtracting the unabsorbed DOX in the supernatant solution during the entire drug loading processes from the initial amount of DOX added. The MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles without loaded DOX were prepared as described above without the addition of DOX and remove of the adsorbed drug molecules.

To evaluate the redox responsive release, a sample of 3mg MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX was dispersed in 9mL volumes of different release media: pH 5.0 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS, pH 5.0 PBS with 10mM GSH, and pH 7.4 PBS with 10mM GSH, followed by shaking at 130rpm (37°C). Subsequently, at specified times, 3mL samples of release solution were removed and replaced with an equal volume of fresh release fluid. The release of DOX from the buffer solution was monitored at 480nm after the centrifugation at 5000rpm for 5min.

The MSNs-SS-CDPAA sample without loaded DOX was dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS with 10mM GSH and shaken at 130rpm and 37°C. After shaking for 0h, 1h, 2h, 4h, 6h and 9h, the sample was centrifuged, washed with distilled water and then redispersed in water to measure the zeta potential. In addition, 2mg MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles were dispersed in 4mL pH 7.4 PBS with 0mM or 10mM GSH. After the shaking at 37°C for 6h, the samples were centrifuged, and the supernatants were collected. Then the supernatants with different concentrations of GSH were observed under UV light at 360nm and then underwent fluorescence spectrophotometric analysis to obtain the emission spectra.

12mg MSNs-SH and MSNs-SS-CDPAA without loaded drug was dispersed in 3mL distilled water and pH 7.4 PBS by sonication to obtain a 4mg/mL dispersion solution. At different times, the photographs were taken with a Canon digital camera to evaluate the stability and dispersity of MSNs-SH and MSNs-SS-CDPAA.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Netherlands) was used to prove the structure and morphology of the mesoporous nanoparticles. Surface area analyzer was used to measure Nitrogen adsorption–desorption at −196°C (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, BeiJing, China). TGA-50 (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to carry out the thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) at the heating rate of 5°C/min under a nitrogen flow of 40mL/min. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FT-IR) spectra was measured on a Bruker IFS (Switzerland) using the KBr pellet technique. The zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameters were measured using a Nano-ZS90 Nanosizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The fluoresce spectrum of the as-prepared CDPAA sample solution was measured on a Hitachi F-4500 (Tokyo, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al Kα X-ray source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out by using a Rigaku Geigerflex X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Denki, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5405Å) and a step width of 0.02°.

The A549 cells were cultured in DMEM culture medium containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The cellular uptake of MSNs, MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX samples by A549 cells was investigated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cells were seeded onto 24-well plates with a sterile round glass dish at a density of 1×105 cells/well and incubated for 24h. Then MSNs, MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX nanoparticles at a final concentration of 25μg/mL were added to the corresponding cell wells. After incubating for 2h at 37°C, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with cold PBS three times and fixed with 4% formaldehyde. Subsequently, DAPI was used to stain the nuclei and the fixed cells were observed using a Leica DM-6000 CS microscope (Leica Instruments Inc., Wetzlar, Germany).

The cytotoxic effects of blank MSNs-SS-CDPAA and DOX-loaded samples on A549 cells were evaluated by MTT assay. The cells were seeded on 96-well plates at a density of 2×104 cells per well. After incubation for 24h, the culture medium was removed and fresh DMEM containing serial concentrations of DOX (0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0μg/mL) formulations were added. This was followed by the addition of a blank MSNs-SS-CDPAA carrier with a concentration equal to that of MSNs-SS-CDPAA in MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX. After an incubation of 24h, 50μL MTT solution (2mg/mL MTT in PBS, pH 7.4) was added to each well and the plate was incubated for further 4h. After incubation, the culture medium containing MTT was removed by rinsing with fresh PBS. Then the intracellular formazan crystals were extracted by adding 150μL DMSO into the plate. The absorbance was measured at 570nm using a microplate reader (SpectraMax M3, Molecular Devices, USA).

The synthetic procedure for CDPAA is illustrated in Scheme 2 . The PAA solution was first pretreated with 1M APS for 1h to obtain oxygen-rich material. Then the starting solution obtained was transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10h. Finally, the larger carbon particles were removed by centrifugation and the supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water and freeze-dried to obtain fluorescent CDPAA. As can been seen from Fig. 1 , the CDPAA without the addition of APS was almost colorless under visible light, while the color of CDPAA became pale yellow when the added volume of APS was more than 0.5mL. Also, the fluorescence intensity of CDPAA gradually increased with an increase in the added volume of APS when irradiated by UV light at 360nm. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDPAA was not significantly different, when the added volume of APS was 1.5mL and 5mL. Excessive oxidation might disrupt the structure of CDPAA and the added volume of APS was fixed at 1.5mL. So, 1.5mL was chosen as the added volume of APS.

As shown in Fig.2 A, the as-prepared CDPAA was uniform, with well dispersed nanoparticles and had a narrow particle size distribution with the average diameter being at 2–4nm. The XRD pattern of CDPAA (Fig.2B) showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ=21.4° attributed to the amorphous carbon phase [22]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig.2C) revealed the presence of lots of carbonyl groups on the surface of CDPAA. In addition, the zeta potential of CDPAA was −27.2mV, which suggested that the carbonyl groups could be grafted onto the surface of the terminal amino group of MSNs. The XPS spectra showed that the C, O and N element percentages were 72%, 27% and 1%, respectively.

In Fig.3 A, the fluorescence spectra of CDPAA showed a maximum excitation at 450nm and a maximum emission at 370nm. Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDPAA were studied to evaluate the fluorescence characteristics of CDPAA under different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig.3B. The PL emission spectrum of CDPAA was an excitation wavelength-dependent and the emission peak shifted from 450nm to 525nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 360nm to 480nm. This result indicated that the CDPAA might emit different fluorescence when excited under different excitation wavelengths.

The synthetic process of MSNs-SS-CDPAA with CDPAA as the gatekeeper connected by disulfide bonds is described in Scheme 1. Firstly, the MSNs-SH nanoparticles were prepared by a post-grafting method using MPTMS as the alkyl coupling reagent, and then reacted with Py-SS-NH2 to obtain MSNs-SS-NH2 by a disulfide bond exchange reaction. Finally, CDPAA was covalently modified on the surfaces of MSNs by an amidation reaction. The average diameter of MSNs nanoparticles was 120nm as shown in the TEM image (Fig. 4 ). An array with a well-ordered mesoporous network was observed in the MSNs from the TEM image.

The isotherms and pore size distribution curves were measured by N2 adsorption–desorption analysis (Fig. S1). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area (SBET), Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution (WBJH) and total pore volume (Vt) are summarized in Table 1 . The SBET and Vt of pure MSNs were calculated to be 1076m2/g and 1.59cm3/g, respectively, and the WBJH was about 3.0nm. After thiol modification, the WBJH of MSNs-SH was reduced to 2.7nm, which indicated that the internal mesoporous were not blocked after grafting thiol groups onto the surface of MSNs.

The successful synthesis of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was examined using a variety of methods. A comparison of the zeta potentials of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA is shown in Fig.5 A. The zeta potential of MSNs-SH had a negative value of −21.1mV. After MSNs-SH reacted with Py-SS-NH2, the zeta potential of MSNs-SS-NH2 was reversed to +39.7mV. After grafting of CDPAA, the zeta potential of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was reversed again to −32.8mV, which indicated that MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles had been successfully synthesized [23]. The FT-IR spectra of MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA are exhibited in Fig.5B. Compared with MSNs-SS-NH2, the appearance of the peak at 1717cm−1 was attributed to the carboxyl group of CDPAA, which indicated the formation of MSNs-SS-CDPAA. TGA of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA was performed to estimate the amount of grafted CDPAA in MSNs-SS-CDPAA. The TGA curves of MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA are shown in Fig.6 A. The weight loss from MSNs-SH, MSNs-SS-NH2 and MSNs-SS-CDPAA was 78.7%, 75.6%, and 71.6%, respectively. Compared with MSNs-SS-NH2, an additional weight loss of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was about 4%, which could be ascribed to the removal of CDPAA on the surface of silica. Thus, the grafting degree of CDPAA on the surface of MSNs was estimated to be 4% [24,25]. As shown in Fig.6B, the MSNs-SS-CDPAA in the bright field was an almost white suspension, while the sample had a blue fluorescence under irradiation by UV light at 360nm. These results indicated that CDPAA had been successfully grafted onto the surface of MSNs-SS-CDPAA.

To evaluate the redox responsive drug release of the MSNs-SS-CDPAA, MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX samples were dispersed in different release media: pH 7.4 PBS, pH 5.0 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS with 10mM GSH and pH 5.0 PBS with 10mM GSH, respectively. The drug release was studied in pH 7.4 PBS to simulate the release behavior of DOX in blood and normal tissues. Also, the drug release in pH 5.0 PBS was studied to evaluate the release of DOX in lysosomes after endocytosis. As shown in Fig. 7 , only 15% DOX was released from MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX into released fluids in the absence of GSH in pH 7.4 PBS for a period of 24h, indicating the good capping efficiency of disulfide-linked CDPAA as a gatekeeper. In contrast, in the presence of GSH, the cumulative percentage release of DOX was markedly increased to ca. 35% in pH 7.4 PBS within 24h. In addition, the release rate was also markedly increased in pH 5.0 PBS in the presence of GSH. The release rate was relatively low in pH 7.4 PBS due to the stronger electrostatic interaction between DOX and silica compared with that in pH 5.0 PBS [26]. In addition, MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX also showed redox-responsive drug release property in presence of 10mM dithiothreitol (DTT) as shown in Fig. S2. The release rate of DOX was markedly improved in the presence of DTT regardless in pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 PBS. This result indicated that other reduced substance could also trigger the drug release from MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX which was consistent with the published work [27,28]. These results illustrated that the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited high redox-responsive drug release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of redox-responsive drug release
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the mechanism of redox-responsive drug release, MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles were dispersed in pH 7.4 solution in presence of 10mM GSH at 37°C. At predetermined times, samples were centrifuged, washed with water and then redispersed in distilled water to measure their zeta potentials. As shown in Fig.8 A, the zeta potential of MSNs-SS-CDPAA increased from −34.1mV to −15.8mV with an increased redox reaction time in the presence of 10mM GSH, which indicated that the disulfide-linked CDPAA had been removed from the surface of MSNs. Also, the zeta potential remained almost unchanged after the addition of GSH for 6h. In addition, the supernatant fluorescent solutions were collected after centrifugation, and the PL intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength at 360nm as shown in Fig.8B. The PL intensity of the supernatant solution was relatively weak in the absence of GSH. However, the PL intensity was markedly increased with the addition of 10mM GSH. These results demonstrated that the disulfide-linked CDPAA could be separated from MSNs attributed to that the disulfide bonds in MSNs-SS-CDPAA could be cleaved in the high concentration of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The advantage of MSNs-SS-CDPAA after modification of CDPAA
ce:section.ce:para: Although MSNs are promising materials for stimuli-responsive drug delivery, the aggregation and hemolytic characteristics of MSNs under physiological conditions have limited their applications [29]. In our study, carboxyl-abundant CDPAA was used not only to control drug release and track the carriers in vivo, but to increase the dispersity and stability of MSNs. The hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of MSNs and MSNs-SS-CDPAA was measured in distilled water (Fig.9 A). The diameter of MSNs was 178nm with a PDI of 0.144, larger than that observed from the TEM image. The TEM image provided the size of the carrier in a dried state without a hydration layer, while the Dh was measured under aqueous conditions. The Dh of MSNs corresponds to the diameter of the silica core plus the thickness of the hydration layer on the surface of MSNs [8]. The Dh of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was 212nm and the PDI of MSNs-SS-CDPAA (0.076) was smaller than that of blank MSNs, indicating that MSNs-SS-CDPAA had a narrow particle size distribution and was well dispersed.

In order to evaluate the stability of nanoparticles, 15mg MSNs and MSNs-SS-CDPAA were respectively dispersed in 5mL deionized water and pH 7.4 PBS. As shown in Fig.9B, although MSNs were stable in deionized water, MSNs underwent flocculation and sedimentation after dispersion in pH 7.4 PBS. In contrast, the dispersion of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was markedly improved after grafting CDPAA on the surface of MSNs. MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanoparticles can remain stable in deionized water and pH 7.4 PBS for 12h. The excellent dispersity of MSNs-SS-CDPAA could be attributed to the surface electrostatic repulsions and steric hindrance of hydrophilic CDPAA and nanoparticles. In addition, the hemolytic behavior was used to evaluate the biocompatibility of MSNs and MSNs-SS-CDPAA. The percentage hemolysis of MSNs-SS-CDPAA was markedly reduced from 45.9% to about 2% after modification of CDPAA at a high concentration of 1000μg/mL as shown in Fig. S3. Therefore, the MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanocarrier exhibited long-term stability under physiological conditions and a reduced percentage hemolysis, which helped produce an increased permeation and retention (EPR) effect by prolonging the circulation time.

Effective cell internalization and localization of nanocarriers and the drug release from nanocarriers in cells plays a significant role in their application in biomedical fields. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was employed to investigate the cellular uptake of MSNs-SS-CDPAA and intracellular drug release by imaging CDPAA and DOX. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , after incubation of A549 cells with bare MSNs for 2h, only the blue fluorescence of cell nuclei stained by DAPI was observed under an excitation wavelengths of 408, 488 and 561nm, indicating that the bare MSNs did not have the ability to reflect laser signals with a specific laser beam. In contrast, when A549 cells were incubated with fluorescent MSNs-SS-CDPAA, the cells emitted blue, green and red fluorescence following excitation at 408, 488 and 561nm, respectively. In addition, the orange fluorescence in the merged image was also found mainly in the cell cytoplasm, suggesting that the MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanocarriers could be taken up by A549 cells. In addition, the A549 cells were incubated with DOX–loaded MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX to evaluate the intracellular drug release behavior. The green fluorescence was mainly present in the MSNs-SS-CDPAA under an excitation wavelength of 488nm, and was mainly localized in the cell cytoplasm. However, the red fluorescence was reflected by both the released DOX and the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX nanocomposites under an excitation wavelength of 561nm. Also, the red fluorescence distributed in the nucleus was more distinct compared with the green fluorescence, which indicated that the DOX molecules released from the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX eventually became localized in the nucleus to kill cancer cells. Thus, considering the intriguing photoluminescence of MSNs-SS-CDPAA, it might be a potential candidate for controlled drug delivery, bioimaging, tracking and biomedical areas.

To confirm the feasibility of the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX system for producing an intracellular therapeutic effect, MTT assay was used for testing of the viability of A549 cells in the presence of various concentrations of free DOX, MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX. Free DOX, MSNs-SS-CDPAA and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX were incubated with A549 tumor cells for 24h and 48h. As shown in Fig. 11 , no obvious cytotoxicity of the empty MSNs-SS-CDPAA towards A549 cells was observed at the tested concentrations for 24h and 48h, demonstrating its good biocompatibility in vivo. A549 cells incubated with free DOX and MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX showed DOX time- and concentration-dependent cytotoxicity at a series of DOX concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 10μg/mL. Compared with free DOX, the cytotoxic effect of MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX was slightly lower at 24h which might be attributed to the incompletely release of DOX from MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX inside cells after uptake of the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX nanocomposite. In addition, the MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited an increased therapeutic effect on cancer cells with an increase in the incubation time up to 48h owing to the fact that the amount of DOX released increased gradually. Also, the cytotoxicity of MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX was almost identical to that of free DOX at the same concentration after incubation for 48h, which could be explained by the fact that the drug had been almost completely released from the nanocarriers to induce cell death. As a result, taking into consideration the biocompatibility and photoluminescence property of the nanocarriers, we concluded that the MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanocomposites had the potential to be used for drug delivery into cancer cells to ablate tumor cells as well as bioimaging in many biomedical areas.

In summary, carboxyl-abundant carbon dots (CDPAA) with a diameter of 3nm were prepared by a hydrothermal polymerizing method. The carboxyl-abundant CDPAA were attached to the outlet of the MSNs by disulfide bonds and served as the gatekeepers to entrap drug molecules within the mesopores and the fluorescent marker tracked the carrier behavior during cancer treatment. The DOX loaded MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX had a relatively high drug loading up to 13.1%. The in vitro release results indicated that the prepared MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited redox-responsive drug release in both pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 PBS. The redox-responsive release mechanism was also studied. The MSNs-SS-CDPAA exhibited good biocompatibility and stability compared with bare MSNs. The cellular uptake result showed that MSNs-SS-CDPAA could emit blue, green and red fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths. Furthermore, MSNs-SS-CDPAA/DOX exhibited a high cellular uptake as shown by CDPAA imaging and an excellent therapeutic effect on cancer cells by MTT assay. The MSNs-SS-CDPAA nanocarriers were developed and found to have great potential for redox-responsive drug release and real-time imaging to monitor the carrier behavior simultaneously during cancer treatment.
We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81473165) and the Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of Drug Preparation Design & Evaluation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. LZ2015068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.08.033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Int. J. Radiat. Oncol. Biol. Phys.
Int. J. Radiat. Oncol. Biol. Phys.
